story:
Humanity finally built a lasting base on the Moon in 2023. Only then, after a few cautious hops across the dust, did we find the thing that changed everything: the Library. Within three years of founding Moon Base Selene, a walker searching for ice stumbled on the first entrance. A month of surveying uncovered a dozen more. After that, the world we thought we knew split open.

The Library wasn’t a single room but a network of vaults and portals. The tech inside wasn’t the creators’ best; it felt tuned to what a young, stumbling species could use. And what did we reach for first? Weapons. They were shockingly easy to reverse-engineer. The United States, sagging under decades of stop-start recession, suddenly leapfrogged everyone. While the rest of the world flew jets, America could pick six targets a second from orbit and burn them down with high-powered lasers. With better weapons, faster computers, and medicine ahead of anything else on Earth, America won. In the shaken calm afterward, a single government rose: the United World States. Here’s to the White, Blue, and Green.

Only then did our best minds tackle the Library’s computers. They were harder. We didn’t know how to talk to them. We broke things, maybe lost data, although other vaults seemed to hold backups. Piece by piece, we fit it together. The answers were sweeping. The big mysteries—how matter works, where life comes from, what time is—settled into place. And then came the part we didn’t want: according to the Library’s records, there was no higher power behind us; the “evil” we feared wasn’t original sin, but an external, ruthless enemy; and the end of our world had a clock on it—not a fixed date, but a countdown triggered when we woke the system: roughly five centuries from first contact, give or take a century.

The Library wasn’t a gift from a kind guardian. It was a field kit from a people at war. The “Creators,” as we named them, had used Earth as a lab and a trap. First, they cleared the board—goodbye, dinosaurs. Then they seeded the planet with violent, adaptable lines, betting on intelligence to emerge. We won that grim race. The next move was simple and sharp: give us enough tech to make us noticeable, and force us to fight to live.

We weren’t a chosen species. We were a weapon. The Creators made us dangerous enough that their enemies, burning along in pursuit, couldn’t ignore Earth. The enemies would find us, be compelled to crush us, and in doing so slow down or divert, buying the Creators time. A decoy with teeth.

So here we are, watching the sleek black ships—what we call the Moon’s Fleet—in the Library’s deep-range projections, creeping closer year by year, vector by vector. Every year turns into a frantic, global effort: building defenses, hardening orbits, expanding off-world, trying new shields, new drives, new medicines. We hope for a miracle and plan for a siege. We know how the odds look. But the race has finally become what it always was: not a scramble for power, but a fight to prove we are more than a tool someone else left behind.

Welcome to the real human race.
